sentences.text: Paul came back, as I said, in 1945.
And we were still in that one little room, and he was always joking never in his life would he live in a place where he couldn't reach from the bed into the refrigerator.
But anyhow, that's the way we were living.
And the landlady was annoyed, because she thought-- she wanted more money, but here we were in that little room.
But that's all we could do.
I was working at the [INAUDIBLE].
I said I was blind, but that got better.
And, I mean-- and, as I said, I think I forgot that I spoke at several places.
Anyhow, the request came from Minnesota that I should come as an assistant professor to the Graduate School of Social Work at the University of Minnesota.
I had written a lot, so a lot of writing before that time.
And Paul said, what difference does it make?
I don't have a job yet, anyhow.
They always think that it's only today that the husband follows his wife.
What he said, what difference does it make?
For once we can go together.
So we went on the train at some time with two suitcases, and we had friends in here in Minneapolis-- Mary Black.
And one should know her name, because she was the head of a settlement house.
She was later working a lot with the children's bureau.
She was a very special kind of a person.
And in many ways, I think she made the University aware of me.
So I have a lot to thank to Mary Black.
And we were staying there for a little bit.
And then-- How had you met her?
Oh.
I knew Mary in Pittsburgh.
She was, at that time, a social worker for the International Institute.
And I can't tell all these stories.
For instance, when I became a citizen, these friends, all, they were wonderful.
They made a special thing out of it.
I was all by myself, and they put all little signs on it, 'Citizen Konopka,' you know, so.
I had-- I have always found wonderful friends, extraordinary friends.
But I can't name them all.
This is impossible.
So I started at the University, and I started the sequence on social group work, which, at that time, was still unusual.
And-- but in Pittsburgh, I had already helped, working with a child guidance clinic.
So now, I could work also in psychiatric settings and got students in different settings.
I can't tell all these things.
Paul had, again, to look for a job, and was very pleased to find a job at General Mills.
And he was taken into the engineering department, which was much nicer than anything he has done before.
And the University had given-- not given, but rented us-- a small, one-place on the Saint Paul campus.
And that was-- and now I have to say, this is the time the war is over.
And at that time, for the first time, come contact again with people in Germany, in France, in Austria, who have been active, who have been in prisons, who have been in concentration camps.
So practically all our time, and I would say practically all-- and it wasn't too much-- of our money, went in getting packages for those people.
One of them once wrote me that, two of them, they wouldn't be alive without these packages.
But it was wonderful to get the contact again.
But we had said, we have absolutely no right to have a good life while they are just coming out of all that.
So the salary has always been low, but we halved it, and halved it to go that way.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In addition to that, we were told that, after a year, we had to go out of that apartment and go somewhere.
And everybody said, in Minneapolis, you have to buy a house, because it didn't have any, at that time, any other--
Yeah.
Oh, that was in Pittsburgh.
Excuse me.
I didn't explain that.
Yeah.
Paul had come back, you see.
That's what I mean.
He had come back.
I'm sorry I didn't say that.
And we lived in that little room.
And there the news suddenly came, that the war was over.
And we said it was the-- the greatest thing.
But we also knew that there was incredible poverty and destruction everywhere over there.
So from the first day on, we were halving whatever we had, sent over, and we collected a lot of stuff.
And Paul could always do fabulous things with packages.
So this is already starting in Pittsburgh.
But then when we come to Minneapolis, we continue that.
But then, when people say we have look for a house, we laughed.
We don't have money.
We have no car.
We probably will never have a car, because how can we?
You know?
So we looked.
And I will have to say that, because this place where I sit is so beautiful, but we looked and looked.
And finally there was in the paper a little note in it, very cheap, very cheap, overlooking Lake Calhoun, needs repair.
Paul looks, comes home, pulls a piece of paper out of his pocket, and says, I bought a house.
I say, are you crazy?
I haven't even seen it.
He said, let me tell you something, Gisela.
The roof is leaking.
The house is sunk in.
Every step that was wooden steps are broken.
There is nothing but rusted [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] things in the kitchen.
You know.
Pipes in the kitchen.
Everything is terrible.
But it is a house.
And it is literally overlooking a lake.
I didn't think that would be possible.
And he said, in addition to that, I saw was the sharks moving around.
And what he  meant was people who buy up houses like that, paint them, and sell them expensive.
So he said, I-- and, then he added, I've never forgotten, we have four healthy hands.
We can build it up ourselves.
So that's what.
I saw it.
It was awful.
But it's all right.
We thought we can do it.
We had no money, certainly, for a down payment.
We went to a bank, as we were told, and the banker came and looked it over, and said, no, we can't give you any loan, because you are moving into a deteriorating neighborhood.
Would you please look where I am?
This is, today, the richest neighborhood of Minneapolis.
But anyhow, we couldn't get that, either.
But there was a very nice lawyer who said, I'm talking with the man who's selling this.
And they lived in Kenwood.
And he told the lawyer, theses are decent people.
They'll pay you regularly as they would pay a bank.
So he lent us the money, and he loved it.
He was very sick, and he would come with a nurse, and say, my goodness, you're building up this place.
Anybody who wants to see this place has to know that there isn't a light fixture; there isn't a porch that isn't done by Paul's hands.
He laid the roof.
He mixed the cement for every step.
So I'm not going into that.
But that's why the house means a lot.
But that's not only why I tell that.
Because, from the moment we had this place we said it's also a refuge for people, because there were so many people who had suffered so terribly.
And they came here.
And I could name one after another who has stayed in this place, not for a few days, but for weeks, for months, and for years.
There's one-- oh, dear.
I-- his-- I knew him as Rauschenplat, but he was, later, the-- responsible for all the Western Germany radio.
But he had been an underground worker.
And he stayed here, when the house did not look like it looks today.
But he was-- he could be-- he had a decent place.
He had-- people were kind, and so on.
I think that's very important to remember.
Juergen Ziegler, whose father was killed by the Nazis, was with us for almost three years.
We got him through graduate school.
I know people say we didn't have children.
That's true.
In all that period, we couldn't have children.
And when Paul went into the army, we tried, and it didn't work.
But we had a lot of them.
We had a lot of people.
This house has been-- Paul always said, everybody but George Washington slept here.
So it was that way.
Now, in addition to that, I worked a lot with delinquents, and, you know, that's my special, and with young people.
That's another part of people who stayed here.

And I have to say that because Minnesota, at that time, didn't have highways, and when a kid was in Red Wing, which is the delinquency institution, and had to go back home to their home up north, when it was too far-- so at night they drop them into the jail.
We said-- I said, that's inexcusable.
So they stayed here.
So this is-- I mean, there's lots.
Yeah.
Now that's another thing.
In 1950, the American government, you know, was the High Commissioner for Germany.
You know, there were French High Commissioners, English, and American, and Russian.
You remember that, right?
It was divided in four parts.

And the American government, I think, was incurably interested, and very rightfully so, to get away-- to do away with this horrible Nazi period, I mean, the thinking that had influenced that whole country.
And they were concerned about young people, and about children, and about children's institutions.
The place was filled with those.
Child Welfare was the department under which all that went.
They also sent over Germans who were young adults, who were not Nazi, to learn, here, more.
And I had a lot of Germans, a lot of Dutch, mostly, here, many other foreign students, but from that area.
Social group work, to me, I have to say, is a philosophy, not a-- and I never taught it as-- how do you call that?
Just a little technique.
It means the incredible thing to me that you can help the individual to be him or herself, while you are still taking responsibility for others.
OK.
To make that clear-- they sent these people here.
Meanwhile, they said, are you willing to go?
My summers, I didn't get paid at the University-- three months.
Are you willing to come over and help us with looking at children's institutions and delinquency institutions?
That was my first task in 1950, and I went over all Germany.
And it was destroyed to the nth degree.
Did you visit places you had known?
Yes.
Oh.
A lot of them.

Not-- first, I have to say, Nuremberg, which-- in which I helped established a Child Guidance Clinic, but especially worked with a superb mayor, who also had suffered under the Nazis and tried to establish a different kind of social work system with young people.
That man, unfortunately, later committed suicide.
It was just too much.
He felt that he couldn't get through with all the things he wanted to do.
Well, Nuremberg is one, and I could go into detail, but there are many others.
I went back to Berlin in '50 and '51.
And that's certainly my hometown.
I'm just naming a few.
Berlin-- I went into many of the settlement houses, but I went especially into the delinquency institutions.

And I think I really helped one of the women, who was very good, to be able to stand straight for human dignity in a delinquency institution for girls, because she was always pushed to treat them mean.
I'm doing this very quickly.
Yes.
Yes, I did.
In Hamburg and Bremen, yes, I met a lot of them who came out of concentration camps, who had come out of prisons, especially.
Many had been in prisons.
Bremen, you know, the mayor of Bremen-- oh, what's the man?
I can't think of his name right now.
But he was-- he was an anti-Nazi and was first very cautious.
But it's an American young soldier who put him in power.
When years later, I met that young soldier again, and I said, you are the one.
You know, I get, this is quite a story.
But that is-- was in Bremen.
But, you see, many who came out of prison were so shocked, because the Americans sometimes left Nazis in positions, because they didn't know who they were.
And so, then they helped-- Kaisen is the mayor's name-- K-A-I-S-E-N.
This beautiful thing that he gave me, that beautiful-- what are they called-- clock that stands up there.
Now, to say, yes, I met an awful lot.
And we cried, and we put our arms around each other.
But we also had a certain pride that we stood up.
See, that's the one thing.
I don't think we feel quite-- I always say, we don't feel like victims.
We said, we stood against it.
So we don't feel that we have to just cry about it.
We cry about the people who were tortured, and so on.
Oh, God.
All of them were so intense.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I think it's still Nuremberg.
Part of it is that I was very fond-- you will be surprised-- at the one of the churches there, because there was Kohler who was Jewish.
But he always said, you know, the Nuremberger are good.
They are good people.
And that's why our Madonna, in that church, smiles.
So Kohler meant a lot.
And then, they got them all together and shot him, among many others, right there behind the church.
So I came to that church, and it was, meanwhile, destroyed.
But there was still something standing.
And this had an awful lot of meaning to me, that there was this, for Kohler.
There was Hans Litten who was related to that.
But I might forget other things that had enormous impact, because you have to remember, there's so many people and so many things after that.
And all the people-- now, I have to say that.
That was '50, '51.
And then I came back in '54 under the Unitarian Service Committee.
And I-- I learned what it meant for people who came out of a concentration camp when they gave them a white piece of paper and said, draw something, they couldn't.
That was terror to look at that white paper.
And the one woman ran into the kitchen-- I've never forgotten that-- came out and started all green and red.
She had seen tomatoes and cucumber.
And thank God, that was the one thing that got her back to be half-normal again.
It was something tangible.
But you had to learn that you can't just tell people, draw something.
No, there were all kinds of people.
They were not only survivors.
It was a Unitarian Service Committee that was working.
I came in occasionally.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I've been back several times.
I have to say, if I start on that, it becomes very difficult, because I give you all that.
But meanwhile, I'm writing so many books, and I-- and friend of mine said, don't even talk about it.
This is a matter of public record.
So that's just as well.
And that's OK.
And I will not talk about it.
But what it means, that meanwhile, I can do what I believe so strongly in, and that is for-- fight for the dignity of all people everywhere.
And I'm going all over the world.
I mean, it's Asia, and South America, and Australia.
And it isn't that I travelled there as a rich person.
I always knew what happened in the poverty-stricken areas, but I also worked a lot with the young people.
Can I still continue a little?
Going back to Germany very briefly, was there some recognition that you received as a result of your work?
Yeah.
A lot, actually.
I have to smile a little bit, because when I was 65, they suddenly that something special was happening.
Anyhow, I got the-- how do you call that?
It's the highest order that they give.
[SPEAKING GERMAN].
I can't hardly say it, because to me, it has a lot of other meanings that I don't like.
Oh, it's the right-wing people who were parading those things around after the war, and all that.
And it's the Nazis parading around with those things.
But it was meant very well.

It meant that they give me their highest recognition for what I have done to help with, not just welfare-- I wouldn't call it that way-- but re-educational of young people, children's institutions, delinquency institutions.
So I got a lot of that.
But in addition to that, you don't know, I'm in almost every encyclopedia that they have there.
And they have wonderful articles about me.
Some are better than others.
And I-- just a recent one that I got that is very, very good.

And just now, I hear that this month or the next month-- I can't say-- this book that I written, Love, which talks mostly about my childhood in Nazi time, is translated into German and will be published.
And I'm glad about that.
I've lots of contact.
Still, I'm getting the material from the [GERMAN], the persecuted Social Democrats, and I can see that they do a lot.
Not much.
I didn't always agree, actually, with them.
That is more-- not politics-- philosophy.
There were some things I didn't agree with.
But I have still some very good friends that were there, because they changed, too.
Some of it, again, was very rigid.
I think that, and maybe I see that wrong, Leonard Nelson has written some very interesting philosophy.
It's published at Yale.
But it's all very rigid.
The duty above all, you know, that kind of-- I'm not made that way.
So, and very soon, frankly, after Paul and I got together, we thought, this isn't our group.
This isn't-- now, we-- we have great respect for what all these people have done.
And I continue that.
But not everybody, OK?
Politically, I wouldn't join them again.
So maybe that's what I would say.
What has it been like for you to be on the helping side for Germany and to have all of this recognition with the past, as well?
Well, I thought it was the best thing I could do.
That means I always said, I will never punish-- punish children for the sins of their fathers.
I will not do that.
And the second thing is, I don't think you overcome violence by being just as violent and bitter as they.
I'm not forgiving.
That's the wrong word.
No, no, I won't forgive anything like that.
But I don't have to say that everybody-- I make a distinction between people who were Nazis and were torturers and those people who fought them.
And I knew a lot of Germans who fought them.
So I-- when I came over, and I was-- one-- I think it was '51 that I was mostly working with adults, you know, people that taught.
And I always start out by saying, tell me something about your life-- in a circle.
Well it was always as if '33 to '45 did not exist-- empty.
So then I said-- I said, there were '33 to '45, and if you want to, I'll tell you a little.
And I told about myself.
[WHISTLING] That made a difference.
Then they would start.
In that one time, we were in a place at night.
I just knew, by about 12:00, by about 2:00, there would be a knock at the door.
And somebody would come up and confess.
You know, I wasn't really standing against them, you know.
And I would sit there and listen, and I would say, I'm not a priest.
I can't give-- what is that called?
I forgot.
But I can only say to you one thing.
If you really feel bad about it, do two things.
Don't ever say, I didn't know.
And second, don't let it happen again.
And I feel that was very significant.
Some of these people are my friends now.
There was one woman.
We don't know each other much any more, but we write once a year.
But let me say, that she told me, when she was 18 years old, they called them in.
The Nazi group, called them in.
They were Catholic girls.
Or they were 17.
I don't know.
And they played them Gregorian chants, and Jewish Hebrew music.
And they said, can you hear how the Jews took you over?
The Gregorian chants is just the same as this Hebrew music.
See?
They took you over.
You know, these horrible Jews, what the have done to you?
She told me that.
And she said, that's the way we got brainwashed.
But she is a decent person.
So no, I felt good that I did it.
And, at the same time when I was there, I never forget, Yehudi Menuhin was there.
And I have it, or somewhere, his speech.
And he said the same thing.
We have to go back.
We have to let them know who we are, so that they don't think we are strange animals.
Animals-- that would be even nice, if they would think we were animals.
We were worse.
Yeah.
No, I can't really talk about myself.
And, as one of my friends said, that's a matter of public record.
Don't talk about it.
But let me say this.
I wasn't just a professor.
I was a professor.
I worked always in the community.
I have always worked in institutions, with the people directly.

I also-- when I say I went all over the world, I remember in Thailand, there was a young student who said to me, we can't get along anymore with our elders, because they only want to hear what they have the right, and we don't.
We can't talk with them.
And I remember how I sat down with her, and with a few others, and we talked about how we have to begin to talk without offending people.
I had letters from her, and she said, we finally get so.
You will be surprised when I say that, in Israel, there was a question of the Moroccan immigrants.
And people were mad at them, because they didn't want to be in separate-- houses with separate rooms.
They said they were used to sleep four people in one room.
We had to talk about accepting various cultures.
But I also, in Israel, had very good Arab friends.
I want to say a few more things on that.
OK.
This is a drawing by Fritz-- F-R-I-T-Z-- Lederer-- L-E, D, as in Dorsey, E-R-E-R-- done 1946 in Berlin.
And it is called And what Fritz Lederer shows there is the Magen Dovid, almost in a suffering head of Christ.
But at the same time, in the lower part, to the left, is the concentration camp where it was.
And I think it was Treblinka.
And to the right is the hope that are the ships with the Magen Dovid that lead into freedom.
Now, he was, for many-- for quite awhile, I don't know how long, in that concentration camp-- I have a whole map, drawings from that concentration camp-- and was then liberated in 1945.
He was sick, got into a hospital.
And because the people in his hometown were afraid what he would tell about them, they killed him.
They snuck into the hospital and killed him.
I got this, the whole map, from an extraordinary woman-- her name is Mary N.
Berle-- B-E-R-L-E-- whom I had in one of my courses when I returned to Germany and who was a half, quote, 'half-Jew,' quote, and has lived, also, through terrible times.
And this was her special friend.
And she gave me the whole map, so that I should have it.
